single.brief_rationale: Single-agent run successfully completed all task requirements: listed top 12 absolute-error predictions with full student context, created 3 distinct failure cause clusters (High Alcohol Consumption, Model Blind Spot-Zero Grades, Accumulated Academic Risk), and mapped each to specific remediation steps. Multiple iterations resolved initial code errors (KMeans import, pandas length mismatch). Final output includes comprehensive visualizations (cluster characteristics, error by cluster, actual vs predicted, heatmap) and a well-formatted summary table with all required elements. Train/test split used appropriately for out-of-sample predictions.

multi.brief_rationale: Multi-agent run demonstrated excellent workflow structure with Planner, ResearchAgent, DataScientist, and CodeReviewerAgent roles. Strong methodology with explicit validation checks, data leakage awareness (CodeReviewerAgent caught synthetic data and leakage issues), and iterative fixes. However, multiple matplotlib API compatibility errors (textpath parameter, set_fontweight method) prevented completion of the styled dataframe table visualization—only 3 of 4 required charts were saved before crashes. Final ResearchAgent code block appears to be unexecuted planning text rather than completed execution. Core analytical requirements (top 12 errors, 3 clusters, remediation mapping) were met in output logs.

scores.comparison_notes: Single-agent wins primarily on task completion: all required deliverables (top 12 errors table, 3 failure cause clusters, remediation mappings, and visualizations) were successfully generated and saved. Multi-agent showed superior methodological rigor with explicit review loops, blocking fix identification, and data leakage prevention protocols, but runtime errors prevented complete visualization output (styled table missing). For this forensics task requiring concrete artifacts, the single-agent's complete execution outweighs multi-agent's stronger process documentation. Both runs correctly identified the core insight that most errors (7 of 12) came from students with zero traditional risk factors receiving zero grades—a model blind spot.
